task_description: Screen each abstract for relevance to: natural language processing for extracting adverse drug events from clinical or biomedical text. Label RELEVANT if the paper applies NLP or text mining to identify, classify, or extract adverse drug event mentions from any clinical or biomedical text source. Label NOT_RELEVANT otherwise.

papers.title: Semi-Supervised Learning for Chemical-Disease Relation Extraction in PubMed Abstracts
papers.abstract: Extracting chemical-disease relations from biomedical literature supports pharmacovigilance. We develop a semi-supervised graph neural network trained on BioCreative V CDR data with 5,000 labelled and 120,000 unlabelled PubMed abstracts. The model achieves F1 0.78 on the test set, outperforming fully supervised BERT and BioBERT baselines. We identify adverse drug event relations as a primary use case and provide a breakdown of recall by relation type.
papers.topic_hint: NLP, chemical-disease relations, PubMed, adverse drug events, semi-supervised
papers.methodology_hint: semi-supervised GNN, BioCreative CDR, F1 0.78, BioBERT comparison, ADE relation extraction
papers.claimed_contribution: Semi-supervised GNN for chemical-disease relation extraction, F1 0.78, targeting pharmacovigilance.

papers.title: Predicting Drug Synergy in Cancer Cell Lines from Genomic and Molecular Features
papers.abstract: Drug combination screening is costly and large-scale computational prediction is attractive. We train a deep neural network on ALMANAC synergy scores and 39 cancer cell line genomic profiles to predict drug pair synergy. The model achieves Pearson r=0.83, outperforming random forest and matrix factorisation baselines. No text or clinical narrative is processed; the model operates entirely on structured genomic and chemical features.
papers.topic_hint: drug synergy, cancer cell lines, genomics, deep learning, ALMANAC
papers.methodology_hint: deep NN, ALMANAC, Pearson 0.83, genomic features — no text processing
papers.claimed_contribution: Deep NN for drug synergy prediction from genomic features, Pearson 0.83, no text involved.

papers.title: Active Learning for Adverse Drug Reaction Detection in Social Media Health Forums
papers.abstract: Social media provides real-time pharmacovigilance signal but labelling is expensive. We apply active learning to prioritise annotation of posts from three health forums (DailyStrength, MedHelp, Twitter) for adverse drug reaction detection. Our uncertainty-sampling strategy reduces labelling cost by 60% while maintaining NLP classifier F1 above 0.80 across 12,000 posts. The approach is transferable to new drugs not seen during training.
papers.topic_hint: active learning, NLP, adverse drug reactions, social media, pharmacovigilance
papers.methodology_hint: active learning, uncertainty sampling, social media ADE, F1 0.80, label-efficient
papers.claimed_contribution: Active learning for ADE detection in social media reducing labelling cost 60% while maintaining F1 0.80.

papers.title: Graph Convolutional Networks for Protein Function Prediction from Interaction Networks
papers.abstract: We apply graph convolutional networks to protein-protein interaction networks and sequence features to predict gene ontology function labels for poorly annotated proteins. Training on STRING-DB and UniProt annotations, the model achieves Fmax 0.61 on the CAFA3 benchmark, outperforming sequence-only and network-only baselines. The work is concerned with protein biology and does not involve clinical text or adverse event detection.
papers.topic_hint: GCN, protein function prediction, PPI networks, STRING-DB, CAFA3
papers.methodology_hint: GCN, PPI + sequence features, Fmax 0.61, CAFA3 benchmark — no clinical text
papers.claimed_contribution: GCN for protein function prediction from PPI networks, Fmax 0.61 on CAFA3, no clinical NLP.

papers.title: Sequence Labelling with BERT for Adverse Drug Event Span Extraction in EHR Notes
papers.abstract: We fine-tune a BERT-based token classifier on 7,500 annotated inpatient EHR notes to extract adverse drug event spans including the drug name, event type, and severity. The model achieves exact-span F1 0.71 and token-level F1 0.84, outperforming BiLSTM-CRF and rule-based baselines. Error analysis shows that rare event types and negated spans are hardest. We release annotation guidelines and a de-identified subset.
papers.topic_hint: BERT, token classification, adverse drug events, EHR notes, span extraction
papers.methodology_hint: BERT fine-tuning, EHR notes, F1 0.71 exact span, BiLSTM-CRF baseline, ADE extraction
papers.claimed_contribution: BERT token classifier for ADE span extraction from EHR notes, exact-span F1 0.71.

papers.title: Transformer Models for Predicting Chemical Compound Toxicity from SMILES Strings
papers.abstract: We train a transformer operating on SMILES representations of chemical compounds to predict acute oral toxicity (LD50), Ames mutagenicity, and hERG inhibition. Evaluated on Tox21 and ClinTox benchmarks, the model achieves AUC 0.88 averaged across tasks. The model processes structured chemical representations, not text narratives, and does not extract adverse events from clinical records.
papers.topic_hint: transformer, SMILES, chemical toxicity, Tox21, hERG, molecular property
papers.methodology_hint: SMILES transformer, Tox21/ClinTox, AUC 0.88, chemical toxicity — no clinical text extraction
papers.claimed_contribution: SMILES transformer for chemical toxicity prediction, AUC 0.88 on Tox21/ClinTox, not NLP on text.

papers.title: Multi-Task Learning for Joint ADE and Drug-Drug Interaction Extraction from Clinical Notes
papers.abstract: Adverse drug events and drug-drug interactions often co-occur in clinical notes and share linguistic patterns. We train a multi-task learning model on 4,800 annotated clinical notes to jointly extract ADE mentions and drug-drug interaction pairs. Sharing representations improves ADE F1 from 0.68 to 0.74 and DDI F1 from 0.63 to 0.69 versus single-task baselines. The model is evaluated on the i2b2 2018 NLP shared task dataset.
papers.topic_hint: multi-task learning, NLP, ADE extraction, DDI, clinical notes, i2b2
papers.methodology_hint: multi-task learning, clinical notes, ADE F1 0.74, DDI F1 0.69, i2b2 2018
papers.claimed_contribution: Multi-task NLP model jointly extracting ADEs and DDIs from clinical notes, ADE F1 0.74.

papers.title: Machine Learning for Predicting Hospital-Acquired Pressure Injury Risk
papers.abstract: Pressure injuries cause significant patient harm and are partially preventable. We train an XGBoost model on structured nursing assessment EHR data from 58,000 admissions to predict pressure injury onset during hospitalisation. AUROC 0.81 is achieved. Braden subscale components, turning frequency, and nutritional status are the strongest predictors. This prediction task concerns injury occurrence during admission, not adverse drug events.
papers.topic_hint: XGBoost, EHR, pressure injury prediction, nursing assessment
papers.methodology_hint: XGBoost, Braden scale features, AUROC 0.81, pressure injury — not adverse drug events
papers.claimed_contribution: XGBoost pressure injury risk predictor from nursing EHR data, AUROC 0.81.

papers.title: Zero-Shot Adverse Drug Event Extraction Using Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Annotated corpora for rare adverse drug events are scarce. We evaluate GPT-4 and Llama-2 in zero-shot and few-shot settings for extracting adverse drug event mentions from pharmacovigilance case reports, discharge summaries, and FDA drug labels. GPT-4 achieves F1 0.67 zero-shot, outperforming previous supervised models on low-frequency event types despite seeing no training examples. Prompt engineering choices substantially affect performance.
papers.topic_hint: LLMs, zero-shot, adverse drug events, pharmacovigilance, GPT-4, few-shot
papers.methodology_hint: GPT-4, Llama-2, zero-shot ADE extraction, F1 0.67, pharmacovigilance case reports
papers.claimed_contribution: GPT-4 zero-shot ADE extraction F1 0.67, outperforming supervised models on rare events.

papers.title: Representation Learning for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Batch Correction
papers.abstract: Integrating single-cell RNA-seq data across experimental batches is a fundamental challenge in computational biology. We develop a variational autoencoder with batch-aware latent space disentanglement, trained on 1.8 million cells from 24 studies. Downstream clustering and trajectory analysis are substantially improved after correction. This is a computational genomics work with no clinical text component or adverse event extraction.
papers.topic_hint: VAE, scRNA-seq, batch correction, representation learning, computational genomics
papers.methodology_hint: variational autoencoder, batch-aware latent space, 1.8M cells, genomics — no clinical NLP
papers.claimed_contribution: VAE for scRNA-seq batch correction improving clustering and trajectory analysis, no clinical NLP.
